items.q: When a wave travels through water, what happens to the water itself?
items.correct: The water oscillates about a fixed point; the wave transfers energy, not matter.
items.misconception: The water moves along with the wave from one place to another.
items.random_wrong: The water turns into wave material and changes state.
items.why: Visually waves look like 'water moving across the surface', so students think water itself travels with the wave, missing that only energy is transferred.

items.q: Why does light bend when it enters a denser medium like glass?
items.correct: Because it slows down — the change in speed at the boundary causes the wavefront to bend (towards the normal).
items.misconception: Because the medium is denser, it physically blocks part of the light and forces it to turn.
items.random_wrong: Because gravity in the glass pulls the light down.
items.why: Students attach refraction to 'density' as a material property without linking it to the change in wave speed and Snell's law.

items.q: When does an electron behave as a wave?
items.correct: In experiments that probe wave properties (e.g. diffraction through a crystal); in particle-like experiments it behaves as a particle.
items.misconception: Electrons are always waves and only look like particles at low energy.
items.random_wrong: Electrons are sometimes waves and sometimes particles depending on the temperature of the apparatus.
items.why: Students seek a single 'true' description; the modern view is that the experimental context selects which behaviour is observed.
It's Longitudinal. “Transverse” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Doubling the frequency of a wave at fixed speed will:
items.why: It's Halve wavelength. “Double wavelength” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Wave speed 340 m/s, wavelength 0.5 m. Frequency in Hz?
items.why: The real figure is 680. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
